speaker: interviewer
text: --go back for a minute and ask you if you know what happened to all those books that your father had in the library at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was before we were herded into the ghetto. One day, a van pulled up to our house and several Germans jumped out with one or two Ukrainians. And they came right into the house. They burst into the house. And that was the one time where they addressed my mother as [GERMAN] Frau, which means 'lady.' It wasn't like a Schwein, you know, or a Jude, a, a dirty Jew. They said [GERMAN] Frau. And they said they wanted to see my father's library.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, how did they know about my father's library? Somebody must have told them. Well anyway, my mother opened the doors to the library. And they went in and meticulously cleaned up, took every book from the shelves. And then they gave my mother a receipt and drove off. So you had no books left.

speaker: interviewee
text: No books left and don't know whatever happened to them. But that's not-- the question that bothers me is, how did they know about the library? And that-- what makes me very sad-- that our friends and neighbors from before turned so very much against us. And I cannot understand that to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you used to sneak out of the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you ever encounter people whom you had known before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And some of them would give me a, a slice of bread or whatever, without money or without anything in exchange. Yes, I did. But not very often. And we had a very interesting system. In the very beginning, when we did sneak out of the ghetto, we tried to do it as a group. Some children would watch from the inside. And when we would come back with our loot, we would divide it. But after a while, it was not a very-- it was very difficult to do. We came with very little. And there was very little to divide. And so we decided whoever took his, his or her turn to get out, they took whatever they-- whatever they obtained to their own family. And this is the way it was. And when I came back and-- without the eggs and with this bloody face, and when my mother came back from work and I told her what happened, again, I had to promise her that I would never do it again. And she just hugged me. She cried. But I-- inside, I never saw my mother cry with tears. But she just hugged me and didn't say anything anymore. A few days after my incident, one of my dearest playmates-- she was so beautiful. I must say that because to this day, I think of her and I see her. She was like an angel. She was very blond, blue-eyed. And people turned their heads because she was so beautiful. She went out through the tunnel and obtained a loaf of bread. And she got caught. And they killed her. And they brought her body back into the ghetto and displayed it. They wouldn't even allow us to bury her. She was there. And I have that picture for the rest of my life. She looked peaceful, with just a little bit of blood on, on her body. And it's something that is very, very traumatic for me to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I cannot say it. And I'll explain why. When we went to Israel in the, in the 1980s, she has a brother there. And he wanted me to answer questions. What happened to his family? I could not-- I know it's a mistake, but I could not bring myself to tell him how his beloved little sister died. And that's why I-- if, for any reason somehow he sees this tape, I don't want him to know that I didn't tell him the whole truth. I told him that she perished with everyone else. But she died a horrible death. She, she, she died for a loaf of bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There were so many incidences that occurred in the ghetto. One time, three of the young workers who were working in the-- I guess, on the ditches or roads tried to escape in broad daylight. And they were caught. And they were brought back to the ghetto. And they erected what do you call it? A hanging-- Gallows.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a, a gallows. And we all had to watch when they hung the three of them. Not one uttered a word. Not one yelled. I mean, they were so stoic, you know. And they died with much of human dignity. In the beginning, when the ghetto formed, we also had one doctor, Dr. Grossfeld. And he had a small hospital, with whatever pharmaceuticals he still managed to get out of his home, I guess. And at first, we had, like, a small hospital. But in one of the roundups-- and roundups occurred almost weekly. A roundup was when the Germans came into the ghetto, any time of day or night, and drove around with trucks and grabbed people randomly. And one of the roundups-- and on one of the roundups, they took away Dr. Grossfeld. And consequently, there was no more hospital. And so people started-- not started, but people died from starvation, from cold, and from sheer hopelessness. And I see corpses piled up on the streets. And the purity of their souls is-- transcends the filth and stench that came with it. It was that-- the innocence and purity of these souls is, is with me to this day. And I do feel that I owe them a great debt. And this is to speak out and to tell their story. And these occurrences happened almost daily. I also forgot to mention before that when we came into the ghetto, shortly afterwards, there was a Judenrat formed. A Judenrat was a group of men, not mostly, but all men, who were our intermediaries with the Germans. The Germans, it was beneath them to talk to a Jew, so they had the committee people. And all their requests came through the Judenrat. Now, the Judenrat had a terribly difficult task because they had to deliver everything the Germans requested. And they had to deliver workers. Some of them were never seen again. And who and how to choose was a very difficult task. And this, this Judenrat stayed with us until the very end. Now, life in the ghetto was getting from bad to worse. Morale was sagging, and people were getting desperate. But somehow, we continued. In my own personal case, I felt still protected by my older sister and by my mother. And somehow, having their love and their caring kept me going. This went on until the next spring. And that, that was 1942. As spring came, our population, the numbers, dwindled. There were probably half of the people in the ghetto at that time. And the Germans decided we had too much space. And so they moved us to an, an-- a smaller ghetto, which was at another part of town, also dilapidated and old. As a matter of fact, that part of town wasn't occupied for several years. There were houses in great disrepair. And that's where we ended up. The only difference was that this new ghetto had an enclosure, again, with a high, wooden fence and barbed wire only on three sides because the fourth side was neighboring the-- a river that separated our town from a village. And so somehow, my mother and my sister and I ended up-- I mean, our assignment was in a little, tiny room in a house right on the river. And my mother, in retrospect, when I think-- she was a very wonderful woman. And somehow, she instilled hope both in my sister and me, in all our neighbors, too. Somehow she gave us strength. I don't know if she had that strength and conviction within her. But on the outside, she seemed very strong and very loving and very hopeful. And she would always talk to my sister and me about the time when we would all be together again and that the war wouldn't last long and Papa would come home. And how she could do it, I don't know. As a matter of, of, of fact, when they took my father away, she turned gray overnight. But I never saw my mother crying, never. Anyway, we ended up in the new ghetto. And everything continued as before. There is one incident. My sister was going to work, and at that time, somehow she was lucky. And she was able to obtain a job knitting articles of clothing for the soldiers on the front, the German soldiers. And that was a very favorable job because they were sitting in a old warehouse and they were protected from the elements, you know, winter and wind and whatever. Well, one day she was ill. And she wasn't able to go to work. And so I took her place because if she didn't go to work, we didn't get her ration. And I took my place in the, you know, in the ghetto early in the morning. And I had her paper. And her friends, you know, showed me where she was supposed to march. And we went into the warehouse. And I sat down at the bench where she was and picked up I don't even remember what she was knitting, a shawl or a scarf, or something. I know it wasn't gloves because I wouldn't have known how to, you know, knit gloves. And anyway, I proceeded to, to knit. And all of a sudden, one of the Germans stood behind me. And I guess he just-- he didn't care, I'm sure, how old I was or whatever. But somehow he didn't-- he didn't like me. And he stood behind and watched for a while. And that made me even more nervous than I was originally. And after a while, he started cursing and screaming at me. And, and, you know, and the more he screamed, the, the slower I was knitting. And finally he got very upset with me and grabbed one of the crochet-- the knitting needles and pushed it into my forefinger. And I passed out. And this is my finger now. I-- it was a bitter lesson. I never took my sister's place again at working.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went-- I waited. And I passed out. And they took care of me there, the-- her friends. And then I marched back home at the end of the day. But there was no, you know, anything to treat my infection. And this is what it is. I just mention it because now my handwriting is so bad. So I can blame it on that. In the late spring of 1942, rumors began to fly in the ghetto that something was up. We really didn't know-- I say we. I didn't know anything. But my mother would come home with different stories. And everyone was getting very apprehensive. There was something going on, but we didn't sense what. We knew it wasn't anything good. My mother desperately tried, while she was working outside building roads and digging ditches, to find a place where she-- we could hide for the rest of the war. And she finally was successful in obtaining two places. She could not get one place for the three of us. Nobody, you know, would hide three people. And so one evening, she and my sister came back from work ecstatic. She did locate two farmers who were willing to take us into hiding. By that time, we knew something was coming and not very-- in the not very distant future. The German guards increased immeasurably. And there was a lot-- a lot of traffic in the ghetto. And everybody sensed that something was happening. And everybody tried either to find a hiding place or naively, they built partitions in their little rooms, thinking they could hide there and the Germans wouldn't find them. I even remember in one of the neighboring houses, a man was trying to find-- build a hiding place in a chimney, you know, which was so unrealistic. But, you know, everyone clings to hope. Well, one day, early-- I guess it was summer by then-- my terrific big sister was getting ready to go to work. We hugged. And my mother was already making plans how we would keep in touch because that evening, my sister was to be picked up and transported to her hiding place. She did go. My mother came home from work by herself. And, in a way, it was a step in the right direction to save our lives. A few days later, my mother came back from work. And until then, she didn't hear anything about my sister, which meant she got to the place where she was supposed to. And a few days later, my mother came home, and we ate our meager meal. And she told me to put on the best clothes I still had and not to forget the jacket that had a little bit of jewelry sewed in. She packed two little bundles. And she kept telling me where we were going. We were going to be hidden by a farmer with whom my mother dealt before the war. We used to buy butter and all sorts of dairy products. And I knew these people. I mean, I knew the, the, the farmer quite well because he lived on a farm across the river on Skobelka. That's the village that we were going to go to. And so after we ate our meager meal and got our clothes-- two sets of everything-- and I remember, I still had some high-- shoes with high-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Laces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with high laces. And I put those on, even though it was summertime. And when it got dark, I held on to my mother's hand. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And we walked into the stillness of the night towards the river. We ended up in the river. And all of a sudden, shots rang out. It was so still, we couldn't move. The shots were sporadic. But we just-- we were pinned there in the bulrushes. And we stayed still. By the time morning came, the shots were not sporadic anymore. They were whizzing by every which way. And we couldn't move. And by that time, many other people from the ghetto did the same thing. They tried to get into the river to save themselves. I don't remember exactly the time, but it seemed-- this lasted for several days. And then we-- we heard babies crying and people screaming and fire and smoke all around us. They did-- that was the time they liquidated the ghetto. We stayed in the river for a number of days. And I was dozing off. And I remember my mother insisted that I eat the bread that she brought with her. And it was soggy. And it tasted so awful. But she insisted I had to stay strong. And she said I had to have my nourishment. And one time, I opened my eyes after I was dozing. And I looked around. And my mother was gone. I felt so guilty and so upset because I felt I let my mother down. I-- she probably tried to wake me, and she couldn't. And she crossed the river by herself. And by that time it was completely quiet. And I waited until the end of the day. And I thought I'd make my way to the farmer because surely she would be there waiting for me. Somehow, I crossed the river. And by the time I did, it was dawn. And I made my way to the farmer's farm. And I think they saw me coming, he, the farmer, and his wife. And they steered me towards the barn and wouldn't even let me into the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked him-- I wouldn't give his name because I think he's still alive, and I have a lot of unanswered questions. But I said, is my mother here? He says, no. I said, well, I'll wait until she comes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, oh no, you will not. And when it gets dark, I want you to leave, or else I'll report you to the authorities. And ironically, he was wearing, like, a set of coveralls. And in his pocket, I saw my father's pocket watch, gold pocket watch with a chain. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But anyway, I asked him-- I said, you promised my mother that you would hide us. And he just shrugged his shoulders. And that was his answer. In the evening, his wife-- he didn't even come back to comfort me or anything. In the evening, his wife came out with an apple and some bread. And she says, you've got to go. And you see, he threatened to denounce me. There was a remuneration for reporting a Jew. It wasn't much money. But there was some money to report a Jew. I left. And I was completely exhausted and confused. And that's where my odyssey really starts. I was bewildered. And I had a terrible sense of guilt, for somehow, I felt I let my mother down. It was harvest time at that time. [CRYING] And there were fields all around Skobelka. And I ended up walking. And the funny thing-- not funny, but curiously, I walked in the main street of the village, without realizing what I did. And somehow-- I don't know, God must have been with me, because no one-- I didn't encounter anyone. And I ended up in the neighboring fields, wheat fields, that were ready for harvest because it was harvest time. And there I spend the next two days trying to sort out what I have to do. By that time, the bread and the apple were gone. And I decided again that he probably refused to let my mother in. And she ran through the forest looking for me. And so I thought, at that time, that I must find my mother. And the only way I could find my mother would be going to the forest. The area where I come from is-- I mean-- I mean, there are forests all over the place. And that's what I decided to do. It wasn't really by choice. That was the only place one could hide. In the very first months of my odyssey, I encountered many stragglers from neighboring ghettos. Apparently, the ghettos were liquidated in our area of Poland about the same time. It was before our high holidays of 1942. There were many encounters in the early days. And we tried to compare, you know, our information and asking for information-- whether or not, perhaps, they've met some of our family or, or friends. I never met anyone who met any of my friends or my family. One time, there were seven of us. And that was about three months after the liquidation of our ghetto-- was already in the fall. There were seven of us sitting at the edge of a forest. And there was one baby, a mother and a baby, and a little boy, and then adolescents, and two older people. We were comparing notes. And all of a sudden, a group of children from the village, from the neighboring village, spotted us. And they started yelling, Jew, we can see you! And they ran back to the village. And we were in a panic. The forest that we were on the edge of was not a good forest to hide in because it didn't have much underbrush. And when you're in a forest where there are trees and no underbrush, you can see so far that there's no place to hide. And so we didn't know what to do. And it was all happening so fast. We ran into the fields. And at that time, there were-- in that area, they have huge haystacks. And we all ran into one haystack, all seven of us. Why we ran into one haystack I, I-- is beyond me. I don't know. But we did. We all hid in that haystack. Well, pretty soon the villagers and some of the kids came back. And they came back with pitchforks. And I'm sure it wasn't very hard for them to zero in on that one haystack that we all were hiding in. And they started-- they made a game of it. With the pitchforks, they started stabbing that haystack. And this went on for hours. And then it got still. I heard screams and all. But that was it. I waited a long time before I made my way out. I must have been right in the middle of the-- of the haystack. And there were six mutilated bodies, their clothing removed, their shoes removed, lined up neatly. Apparently, they didn't count right. They thought they got us all. I'm ashamed to say, I didn't cover them. I didn't try to cover them. [CRYING] I just walked away. I walked all night long that night. And I was still in that forest, where there's-- where there was very little chance for me to hide. And when dawn came, I, I wasn't-- I was still in the same forest. And so I climbed up a tree. And I stayed there. And it was a good thing, too, because somehow, for some reason, there was some kind of activity. And there were Ukrainians and Germans, and I don't know, they had a picnic. I don't know what. But there were all sorts of people. And had they looked up, they could have seen me, because I wasn't really hidden. But I stayed there all day long. I went to the bathroom and everything, right there. And the needles were sticking in my body. But they did not find me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I had many, many close calls. OK, we're going to change the tape now so that you can--

speaker: interviewee
text: God, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: --continue, OK? We're just going to put--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in a new tape. OK?
